758	\subsection{The ambient subquotient induction for the Stacks~00K1 step}
759	\label{subsec:gradedModuleApi}
760	
761	The power-series side of \cref{lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational} is fully isolated
762	in the \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}\) toolkit (\cref{subsec:isRatHilb}). What remains is
763	the \emph{graded-module} side of the Stacks~00K1 induction. Rather than build the
764	kernel and cokernel of multiplication by a degree-one element as graded modules
765	over a smaller quotient ring \(R/(x)\) -- which would force a graded structure on a
766	derived carrier -- we range the induction over \emph{pairs of homogeneous
767	submodules of a single fixed ambient graded module} \(M\). A pair \((N, N')\) with
768	\(N' \le N\) homogeneous submodules of \(M\) stands for the subquotient \(N/N'\); its
769	ambient Hilbert function is the difference \(n \mapsto \dim_\kappa(N \cap M_n) -
770	\dim_\kappa(N' \cap M_n)\). The class of such pairs, equipped with finitely many
771	pairwise-commuting degree-one endomorphisms preserving \(N\) and \(N'\), is
772	\emph{closed} under the kernel and cokernel of one of those endomorphisms, both
773	again presented as ambient subquotient pairs. Consequently no graded quotient ring,
774	graded quotient module, or regrading of a subtype/quotient carrier is ever formed:
775	every object that appears is an ambient family \(n \mapsto N_{\mathrm{aux}} \cap M_n\)
776	of submodules of the fixed \(M\).
777	
778	This subsection records that machinery as a thin layer over existing Mathlib
779	scaffold: Mathlib already provides the notion of a homogeneous submodule
780	(\cref{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}), the homogeneity of a span of
781	homogeneous elements (\cref{lem:ideal_homogeneous_span_mathlib}), the degree-shift
782	of multiplication by a homogeneous element
783	(\cref{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}), the rank--nullity identity
784	(\cref{lem:finrank_range_add_finrank_ker_mathlib}), and the finite-generation
785	transfer along a surjection (\cref{lem:fg_restrictScalars_of_surjective_mathlib}).
786	What is supplied here is the internal direct-sum decomposition of a homogeneous
787	submodule as an ambient family (the split \(G_1\)), the subquotient Hilbert data,
788	the kernel/cokernel closure, the degreewise difference identity, the ambient
789	finiteness transfer, and the induction itself.
790	
791	\emph{Setting for this subsection.} Let \(R = \bigoplus_{n \ge 0} R_n\) be a graded
792	ring whose degree-zero part \(R_0 = \kappa\) is a field and which is generated as a
793	\(\kappa\)-algebra in degree one, and let \(M = \bigoplus_{n \ge 0} M_n\) be a graded
794	\(\kappa\)-vector space (the fixed ambient module) with finite-dimensional
795	components \(M_n\). All submodules \(N, N', \dots\) below are homogeneous submodules
796	of \(M\), and the relevant endomorphisms are \(\kappa\)-linear maps \(M \to M\)
797	raising degree by one -- typically multiplication by a homogeneous element
798	\(x \in R_1\).
799	
800	\subsubsection*{Mathlib dependency anchors}
801	
802	\begin{lemma}[Homogeneous submodule]
803	  \label{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
804	  \lean{Submodule.IsHomogeneous}
805	  \mathlibok
806	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
807	  (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.GradedAlgebra.Homogeneous.Submodule}).}
808	  Let \(R = \bigoplus_n R_n\) be a graded ring and \(M = \bigoplus_n M_n\) an
809	  internally graded \(R\)-module. A submodule \(p \subseteq M\) is
810	  \emph{homogeneous} when it is closed under taking homogeneous components:
811	  \(m \in p\) implies each graded piece \(\mathrm{proj}_n(m) \in p\). Equivalently
812	  \(p = \bigoplus_n (p \cap M_n)\) as a set. Mathlib bundles this into a homogeneous
813	  submodule type providing order and extensionality structure; the ambient internal
814	  direct-sum decomposition \(p = \bigoplus_n (M_n \cap p)\) is supplied by the split
815	  \(G_1\) (\cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep},
816	  \cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq}).
817	\end{lemma}
818	
819	\begin{lemma}[A span of homogeneous elements is homogeneous]
820	  \label{lem:ideal_homogeneous_span_mathlib}
821	  \lean{Ideal.homogeneous_span}
822	  \mathlibok
823	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
824	  (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.GradedAlgebra.Homogeneous.Ideal}).}
825	  Let \(R = \bigoplus_n R_n\) be a graded ring and \(s \subseteq R\) a set each of
826	  whose elements is homogeneous. Then the ideal \(\mathrm{span}\,s\) is a
827	  homogeneous ideal. In particular, for \(x \in R_1\) the principal ideal \((x)\)
828	  is homogeneous, hence \(xM\) and the auxiliary submodule \(N' + x \cdot N\) are
829	  homogeneous; this homogeneity feeds the kernel/cokernel closure
830	  (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker}).
831	\end{lemma}
832	
833	\begin{lemma}[Rank--nullity]
834	  \label{lem:finrank_range_add_finrank_ker_mathlib}
835	  \lean{LinearMap.finrank_range_add_finrank_ker}
836	  \mathlibok
837	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
838	  (\texttt{Mathlib.LinearAlgebra.FiniteDimensional.Lemmas}).}
839	  Let \(\kappa\) be a field, \(V\) a finite-dimensional \(\kappa\)-vector space and
840	  \(\varphi : V \to W\) a \(\kappa\)-linear map. Then
841	  \[
842	    \dim_\kappa(\operatorname{range}\varphi) + \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi)
843	      \;=\; \dim_\kappa V .
844	  \]
845	  This is the rank--nullity identity underlying the degreewise difference identity
846	  \(D_5\) (\cref{lem:graded_degreewise_finrank_diff}).
847	\end{lemma}
848	
849	\begin{lemma}[Finite generation transfers along a surjection]
850	  \label{lem:fg_restrictScalars_of_surjective_mathlib}
851	  \lean{Submodule.FG.restrictScalars_of_surjective}
852	  \mathlibok
853	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Finiteness.Basic}).}
854	  Let \(\varphi : R \to A\) be a surjective ring homomorphism and \(M\) an
855	  \(A\)-module that is finitely generated when viewed as an \(R\)-module via
856	  \(\varphi\). Then \(M\) is finitely generated as an \(A\)-module. Applied to the
857	  surjection \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}] \twoheadrightarrow \kappa[t_0, \dots,
858	  t_{r-2}]\) killing the endomorphism \(x = t_{r-1}\) that annihilates the two
859	  subquotients, this transfers their finite generation down one generator
860	  (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer}).
861	\end{lemma}
862	
863	\begin{lemma}[Multiplication by a homogeneous element shifts degree]
864	  \label{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}
865	  \lean{SetLike.IsHomogeneousElem.graded_smul}
866	  \mathlibok
867	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.GradedMulAction}).}
868	  Let \(\mathcal{A}\) be a graded family acting on a graded family \(\mathcal{M}\)
869	  via a graded scalar multiplication. If \(a \in R\) is homogeneous of degree
870	  \(i\) -- in particular \(x \in R_1\) -- then for \(m \in M_n\) one has
871	  \(a \cdot m \in M_{i+n}\). This is the degree-shifting property that makes \(xM\)
872	  a homogeneous submodule (with degree-\((n+1)\) part \(x \cdot M_n\)) and that
873	  underlies the ambient identity \(x \cdot N \cap M_{n+1} = x \cdot (N \cap M_n)\)
874	  used in the subquotient closure (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker}) and
875	  difference identity (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff}).
876	\end{lemma}
877	
878	\subsubsection*{The internal grading of a homogeneous submodule (split $G_1$)}
879	
880	\begin{lemma}[$G_1$ (independence) -- the family $n \mapsto M_n \cap p$ is independent]
881	  \label{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep}
882	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.homogeneousSubmodule_inf_iSupIndep}
883	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
884	  Let \(M = \bigoplus_n M_n\) be an internally graded \(\kappa\)-module and let
885	  \(p \subseteq M\) be a homogeneous submodule
886	  (\cref{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}). Then the family
887	  \(n \mapsto M_n \cap p\) of \(\kappa\)-subspaces of \(M\) is independent: for each
888	  \(n\),
889	  \[
890	    (M_n \cap p) \;\cap\; \Bigl(\bigsqcup_{m \ne n} (M_m \cap p)\Bigr) \;=\; 0 .
891	  \]
892	  The whole statement lives in the ambient module \(M\): the subspaces
893	  \(M_n \cap p\) are submodules of \(M\), never of a derived carrier \(\smash{\hat p}\).
894	\end{lemma}
895	
896	\begin{proof}
897	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
898	  The components \(M_n\) of the internal decomposition of \(M\) are independent. The
899	  subfamily \(n \mapsto M_n \cap p\) consists of submodules with \(M_n \cap p \le M_n\),
900	  so independence is inherited: a relation among the \(M_n \cap p\) is in particular a
901	  relation among the \(M_n\), forcing each term to vanish.
902	\end{proof}
903	
904	\begin{lemma}[$G_1$ (supremum) -- $\bigsqcup_n (M_n \cap p) = p$ for $p$ homogeneous]
905	  \label{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq}
906	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_inf_eq}
907	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
908	  Let \(p \subseteq M\) be a homogeneous submodule
909	  (\cref{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}). Then
910	  \[
911	    \bigsqcup_{n} (M_n \cap p) \;=\; p .
912	  \]
913	  Together with the independence of
914	  \cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep}, this is the ambient internal
915	  direct sum \(p = \bigoplus_n (M_n \cap p)\), stated entirely inside \(M\) (no
916	  \(\mathrm{DirectSum.Decomposition}\) on the subtype \(\smash{\hat p}\)).
917	\end{lemma}
918	
919	\begin{proof}
920	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
921	  The inclusion \(\bigsqcup_n (M_n \cap p) \le p\) is clear, since each
922	  \(M_n \cap p \le p\). For the reverse inclusion, take \(m \in p\) and write
923	  \(m = \sum_n \mathrm{proj}_n(m)\) with \(\mathrm{proj}_n(m) \in M_n\) using the
924	  internal decomposition of \(M\). Homogeneity of \(p\) gives
925	  \(\mathrm{proj}_n(m) \in p\), hence \(\mathrm{proj}_n(m) \in M_n \cap p\); thus
926	  \(m\) is a finite sum of elements of the \(M_n \cap p\), i.e.
927	  \(m \in \bigsqcup_n (M_n \cap p)\).
928	\end{proof}
929	
930	\subsubsection*{The ambient subquotient class and its Hilbert function}
931	
932	\begin{definition}[Subquotient Hilbert data]
933	  \label{def:graded_subquotientHilb}
934	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.subquotientHilb}
935	  \uses{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep,
936	    lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq}
937	  Fix a graded \(\kappa\)-module \(\mathcal{M}\), i.e. \(M\) together with an
938	  internal decomposition \(M = \bigoplus_n \mathcal{M}_n\)
939	  (\(\mathrm{DirectSum.Decomposition}\ \mathcal{M}\)) whose components
940	  \(\mathcal{M}_n\) are finite-dimensional over \(\kappa\). A \emph{subquotient
941	  datum of length \(r\)} consists of:
942	  \begin{itemize}
943	    \item a pair of homogeneous submodules \(N' \le N \subseteq M\); and
944	    \item a family \(t : \{0, \dots, r-1\} \to (M \to_\kappa M)\) of pairwise
945	      commuting \(\kappa\)-linear endomorphisms, each raising degree by one
946	      (\(t_i(\mathcal{M}_n) \subseteq \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\)), each preserving \(N\)
Let me continue reading the QuotScheme chapter from line 947.
Let me continue reading the QuotScheme chapter and the other chapters.

947	      and \(N'\) (\(t_i N \subseteq N\), \(t_i N' \subseteq N'\)); together with
948	    \item a finiteness witness that \(M\) is a finite module over the polynomial
949	      ring \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\) acting through the \(t_i\)
950	      (\(\mathrm{Module.Finite}\)).
951	  \end{itemize}
952	  The pair \((N, N')\) represents the subquotient \(N/N'\). Its \emph{ambient
953	  Hilbert function} is
954	  \[
955	    \mathrm{hilb}(n) \;=\; \Bigl(\dim_\kappa (N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)
956	      \;-\; \dim_\kappa (N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\Bigr) \;\in\; \mathbb{Q},
957	  \]
958	  the difference of the (finite) \(\kappa\)-dimensions of the ambient intersections,
959	  computed in \(\mathbb{Z}\) and cast to \(\mathbb{Q}\). Since \(N' \le N\) one has
960	  \(N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n \le N \cap \mathcal{M}_n\), so
961	  \(\mathrm{hilb}(n) = \dim_\kappa\bigl((N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)/(N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\bigr)\)
962	  is the dimension of the degree-\(n\) piece of \(N/N'\). The split \(G_1\)
963	  (\cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep},
964	  \cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq}) guarantees that these ambient
965	  intersections assemble the homogeneous submodules \(N, N'\), so the data depends
966	  only on the ambient families \(n \mapsto N \cap \mathcal{M}_n\),
967	  \(n \mapsto N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n\).
968	\end{definition}
969	
970	\subsubsection*{Closure under kernel and cokernel}
971	
972	\begin{lemma}[Kernel/cokernel closure of the subquotient class]
973	  \label{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker}
974	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.subquotient_ker_coker}
975	  \uses{def:graded_subquotientHilb, lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep,
976	    lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq, lem:ideal_homogeneous_span_mathlib,
977	    lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}
978	  Let \((N, N')\) be a subquotient datum (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb}) with
979	  endomorphisms \(t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}\), and set \(x = t_{r-1}\). Form the two new
980	  pairs of homogeneous submodules of \(M\)
981	  \[
982	    K \;=\; \bigl(\,N \cap x^{-1}(N'),\ N'\,\bigr),
983	    \qquad
984	    C \;=\; \bigl(\,N,\ N' + x \cdot N\,\bigr),
985	  \]
986	  where \(x^{-1}(N') = \{\,m \in M : x \cdot m \in N'\,\}\) is the preimage and
987	  \(x \cdot N\) is the image of \(N\) under \(x\). Then:
988	  \begin{enumerate}
989	    \item \(N \cap x^{-1}(N')\) and \(N' + x \cdot N\) are homogeneous submodules of
990	      \(M\), with \(N' \le N \cap x^{-1}(N')\) and \(N' + x \cdot N \le N\), so both
991	      \(K\) and \(C\) are subquotient pairs;
992	    \item the remaining endomorphisms \(t_0, \dots, t_{r-2}\) pairwise commute,
993	      raise degree by one, and preserve both members of \(K\) and of \(C\); and
994	    \item \(x\) annihilates both subquotients: \(x \cdot (N \cap x^{-1}(N')) \subseteq N'\)
995	      and \(x \cdot N \subseteq N' + x \cdot N\).
996	  \end{enumerate}
997	  Consequently \(K\) and \(C\) are again subquotient data, now of length \(r-1\)
998	  (the endomorphisms \(t_0, \dots, t_{r-2}\)), and \(K = \ker x\) and \(C =
999	  \operatorname{coker} x\) on the subquotient \(N/N'\): \(K\) represents
1000	  \(\ker\bigl(x : N/N' \to N/N'\bigr)\) and \(C\) represents
1001	  \(\operatorname{coker}\bigl(x : N/N' \to N/N'\bigr)\). No quotient or subtype
1002	  carrier is constructed; \(K\) and \(C\) are ambient pairs of homogeneous
1003	  submodules of the fixed \(M\).
1004	\end{lemma}
1005	
1006	\begin{proof}
1007	  \uses{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep,
1008	    lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq, lem:ideal_homogeneous_span_mathlib,
1009	    lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}
1010	  \emph{Homogeneity.} The preimage \(x^{-1}(N')\) is homogeneous: if
1011	  \(m = \sum_n \mathrm{proj}_n(m)\) with \(x \cdot m \in N'\), then since \(x\) raises
1012	  degree by one (\cref{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}) the components
1013	  \(x \cdot \mathrm{proj}_n(m) \in \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\) lie in distinct degrees, and
1014	  homogeneity of \(N'\) (split \(G_1\),
1015	  \cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq}) forces each
1016	  \(x \cdot \mathrm{proj}_n(m) \in N'\), i.e.\ \(\mathrm{proj}_n(m) \in x^{-1}(N')\).
1017	  Thus \(N \cap x^{-1}(N')\) is an intersection of homogeneous submodules, hence
1018	  homogeneous. The image \(x \cdot N\) is homogeneous with degree-\((n+1)\) piece
1019	  \(x \cdot (N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\) by
1020	  \cref{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}, and a sum of homogeneous
1021	  submodules is homogeneous (\cref{lem:ideal_homogeneous_span_mathlib} applied at the
1022	  module level), so \(N' + x \cdot N\) is homogeneous. The inclusions
1023	  \(N' \le N \cap x^{-1}(N')\) (as \(x \cdot N' \subseteq N'\)) and
1024	  \(N' + x \cdot N \le N\) (as \(N' \le N\) and \(x \cdot N \subseteq N\)) are
1025	  immediate.
1026	
1027	  \emph{Preservation.} Each \(t_i\) with \(i \le r-2\) commutes with \(x\) and
1028	  preserves \(N, N'\); hence it preserves \(x^{-1}(N')\) (if \(x t_i m = t_i x m \in
1029	  N'\) whenever \(x m \in N'\)), so it preserves \(N \cap x^{-1}(N')\), and it
1030	  preserves \(x \cdot N\) (as \(t_i x N = x t_i N \subseteq x N\)), so it preserves
1031	  \(N' + x \cdot N\). Degree-raising and commutativity are inherited.
1032	
1033	  \emph{Annihilation by \(x\).} For \(m \in N \cap x^{-1}(N')\) one has
1034	  \(x \cdot m \in N'\) by definition, so \(x\) carries the first member of \(K\) into
1035	  its second; and \(x \cdot N \subseteq N' + x \cdot N\) trivially. Thus \(x\) acts as
1036	  zero on \(N/N'\) restricted to \(K\) and on the cokernel quotient \(C\).
1037	
1038	  \emph{Identification with \(\ker x\) and \(\operatorname{coker} x\).} On the
1039	  subquotient \(N/N'\), the class of \(m \in N\) is killed by \(x\) iff
1040	  \(x \cdot m \in N'\), i.e.\ iff \(m \in N \cap x^{-1}(N')\); so \(\ker(x : N/N' \to
1041	  N/N')\) is exactly \((N \cap x^{-1}(N'))/N'\), represented by \(K\). The image of
1042	  \(x\) on \(N/N'\) is \((N' + x \cdot N)/N'\), so the cokernel
1043	  \((N/N')/\operatorname{im} x = N/(N' + x \cdot N)\) is represented by \(C\).
1044	\end{proof}
1045	
1046	\subsubsection*{The subquotient degreewise difference identity ($D_6$)}
1047	
1048	\begin{lemma}[$D_6$ -- subquotient degreewise difference]
1049	  \label{lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff}
1050	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.subquotient_degreewise_diff}
1051	  \uses{def:graded_subquotientHilb, lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker,
1052	    lem:graded_degreewise_finrank_diff, lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep,
1053	    lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq,
1054	    lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}
1055	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Commutative Algebra", \S "Noetherian graded
1056	  % rings", Proposition \texttt{proposition-graded-hilbert-polynomial} (Tag
1057	  % 00K1), proof (read from references/hilbert-serre-algebra.tex, L13939-L13947).
1058	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag 00K1 (degreewise short exact sequence).}
1059	  Let \((N, N')\) be a subquotient datum (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb}) with
1060	  Hilbert function \(h_M\), and let \(x = t_{r-1}\). Let \(h_K\) and \(h_C\) be the
1061	  Hilbert functions of the kernel and cokernel subquotients
1062	  \(K = (N \cap x^{-1}(N'),\, N')\) and \(C = (N,\, N' + x \cdot N)\) of
1063	  \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker}. Then for every \(n\)
1064	  \[
1065	    h_M(n+1) - h_M(n) \;=\; h_C(n+1) - h_K(n).
1066	  \]
1067	  Writing \(Q_n = (N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)/(N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\) for the
1068	  degree-\(n\) piece of \(N/N'\) (so \(\dim_\kappa Q_n = h_M(n)\)), multiplication by
1069	  \(x\) is a \(\kappa\)-linear map \(x : Q_n \to Q_{n+1}\) with
1070	  \(\ker(x : Q_n \to Q_{n+1})\) the degree-\(n\) piece of \(K\) and
1071	  \(Q_{n+1}/\operatorname{im} x\) the degree-\((n+1)\) piece of \(C\); the identity is
1072	  then the degreewise rank--nullity difference \(D_5\)
1073	  (\cref{lem:graded_degreewise_finrank_diff}). Specialising to \((N, N') = (\top,
1074	  \bot)\) recovers the classical \(0 \to \mathcal{M}_n \xrightarrow{x}
1075	  \mathcal{M}_{n+1} \to \mathcal{M}_{n+1}/x\mathcal{M}_n \to 0\).
1076	\end{lemma}
1077	
1078	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: [Stacks Project], Tag 00K1, proof, inductive step (read
1079	% from references/hilbert-serre-algebra.tex, L13939-L13947).
1080	% "Let $\overline{M} = M/xM$. Note that the map $x : M \to M$
1081	%  is {\it not} a map of graded $S$-modules, since it does
1082	%  not map $M_d$ into $M_d$. Namely, for each $d$ we have the
1083	%  following short exact sequence
1084	%  $$
1085	%  0 \to M_d \xrightarrow{x} M_{d + 1} \to \overline{M}_{d + 1} \to 0
1086	%  $$
1087	%  This proves that $[M_{d + 1}] - [M_d] = [\overline{M}_{d + 1}]$.
1088	%  Hence we win by Lemma \ref{lemma-numerical-polynomial}."
1089	\begin{proof}
1090	  \uses{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker, lem:graded_degreewise_finrank_diff,
1091	    lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq,
1092	    lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}
1093	  Since \(N' \le N\) are homogeneous, the split \(G_1\)
1094	  (\cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq}) identifies the degree-\(n\) piece
1095	  of \(N/N'\) with \(Q_n = (N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)/(N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\), a
1096	  finite-dimensional \(\kappa\)-vector space of dimension \(h_M(n)\). Because \(x\)
1097	  raises degree by one and preserves \(N\) and \(N'\)
1098	  (\cref{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}), it induces a \(\kappa\)-linear
1099	  map \(x : Q_n \to Q_{n+1}\).
1100	
1101	  \emph{Kernel.} A class \([m] \in Q_n\) (with \(m \in N \cap \mathcal{M}_n\)) lies in
1102	  \(\ker(x : Q_n \to Q_{n+1})\) iff \(x \cdot m \in N' \cap \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\), i.e.\
1103	  iff \(m \in (N \cap x^{-1}(N')) \cap \mathcal{M}_n\). Hence \(\ker(x : Q_n \to
1104	  Q_{n+1}) = \bigl((N \cap x^{-1}(N')) \cap \mathcal{M}_n\bigr)/(N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\)
1105	  is exactly the degree-\(n\) piece of \(K\), of dimension \(h_K(n)\)
1106	  (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker}).
1107	
1108	  \emph{Cokernel.} The image of \(x : Q_n \to Q_{n+1}\) is
1109	  \(\bigl(x \cdot (N \cap \mathcal{M}_n) + (N' \cap \mathcal{M}_{n+1})\bigr)/(N' \cap
1110	  \mathcal{M}_{n+1})\). Using the ambient identity \((N' + x \cdot N) \cap
1111	  \mathcal{M}_{n+1} = (N' \cap \mathcal{M}_{n+1}) + x \cdot (N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\)
1112	  -- which holds because \(x \cdot N\) has degree-\((n+1)\) piece \(x \cdot (N \cap
1113	  \mathcal{M}_n)\) (\cref{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}) and \(N'\) is
1114	  homogeneous -- the cokernel \(Q_{n+1}/\operatorname{im} x = (N \cap
1115	  \mathcal{M}_{n+1})/\bigl((N' + x \cdot N) \cap \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\bigr)\) is the
1116	  degree-\((n+1)\) piece of \(C\), of dimension \(h_C(n+1)\).
1117	
1118	  \emph{Difference.} Apply the degreewise rank--nullity difference \(D_5\)
1119	  (\cref{lem:graded_degreewise_finrank_diff}) to \(\varphi = (x : Q_n \to Q_{n+1})\),
1120	  with \(V = Q_n\), \(W = Q_{n+1}\):
1121	  \[
1122	    h_M(n+1) - h_M(n)
1123	      = \dim_\kappa Q_{n+1} - \dim_\kappa Q_n
1124	      = \dim_\kappa(Q_{n+1}/\operatorname{im}\varphi) - \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi)
1125	      = h_C(n+1) - h_K(n).
1126	  \]
1127	  This is the \(\mathrm{hdiff}\) hypothesis consumed by \cref{lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq}.
1128	\end{proof}
1129	
1130	\subsubsection*{Ambient finiteness transfer}
1131	
1132	\begin{lemma}[Finiteness transfer for the subquotients]
1133	  \label{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer}
1134	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.subquotient_finite_transfer}
1135	  \uses{def:graded_subquotientHilb, lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker,
1136	    lem:fg_restrictScalars_of_surjective_mathlib}
1137	  Let \((N, N')\) be a subquotient datum of length \(r \ge 1\)
1138	  (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb}), so that \(M\) is finite over
1139	  \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\), and let \(x = t_{r-1}\). Then the two
1140	  subquotients
1141	  \[
1142	    K = (N \cap x^{-1}(N'),\, N'), \qquad C = (N,\, N' + x \cdot N)
1143	  \]
1144	  of \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker} are finite modules over
1145	  \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-2}]\); that is, they are subquotient data of length
1146	  \(r-1\) with a valid \(\mathrm{Module.Finite}\) witness. This is the finiteness
1147	  needed for the inductive hypothesis of \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_isRatHilb}.
1148	\end{lemma}
1149	
1150	\begin{proof}
1151	  \uses{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker,
1152	    lem:fg_restrictScalars_of_surjective_mathlib}
1153	  Since \(M\) is finite over the Noetherian ring
1154	  \(A = \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\), \(M\) is a Noetherian \(A\)-module, so the
1155	  submodule \(N \cap x^{-1}(N') \subseteq M\) is finite over \(A\) and the quotient
1156	  \(N/(N' + x \cdot N)\) of the submodule \(N\) is finite over \(A\); likewise
1157	  \((N \cap x^{-1}(N'))/N'\) is finite over \(A\). By
1158	  \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker} the element \(x = t_{r-1}\) annihilates
1159	  both subquotients, so the \(A\)-action on each factors through the surjection
1160	  \[
1161	    A = \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}] \twoheadrightarrow \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-2}]
1162	  \]
1163	  sending \(t_{r-1} \mapsto 0\). Applying the finite-generation transfer along a
1164	  surjection (\cref{lem:fg_restrictScalars_of_surjective_mathlib}) to this
1165	  surjection turns finiteness over \(A\) into finiteness over
1166	  \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-2}]\). Everything is about finite generation of ambient
1167	  submodules and their quotients; no derived-carrier grading is used.
1168	\end{proof}
1169	
1170	\subsubsection*{The degreewise difference identity}
1171	
1172	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1173	[$D_5$ -- degreewise rank--nullity difference]
1174	  \label{lem:graded_degreewise_finrank_diff}
1175	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.degreewise_finrank_diff}
1176	  \uses{lem:finrank_range_add_finrank_ker_mathlib,
1177	    lem:finrank_ses_additive_mathlib}
1178	  Let \(\varphi : V \to W\) be a \(\kappa\)-linear map between finite-dimensional
1179	  \(\kappa\)-vector spaces. Then
1180	  \[
1181	    \dim_\kappa W - \dim_\kappa V
1182	      \;=\; \dim_\kappa(W/\operatorname{range}\varphi) - \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi).
1183	  \]
1184	  Applied degreewise with \(\varphi = (x : \mathcal{M}_n \to \mathcal{M}_{n+1})\),
1185	  \(V = \mathcal{M}_n\), \(W = \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\) -- so that \(\ker\varphi\) is the
1186	  degree-\(n\) piece \(K_n\) of the kernel subquotient and
1187	  \(W/\operatorname{range}\varphi = \mathcal{M}_{n+1}/x \mathcal{M}_n\) the
1188	  degree-\((n+1)\) piece \(C_{n+1}\) of the cokernel subquotient
1189	  (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff}, via the split \(G_1\)
1190	  \cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq} and the degree shift
1191	  \cref{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}) -- this gives, for every \(n\),
1192	  \[
1193	    \dim_\kappa \mathcal{M}_{n+1} - \dim_\kappa \mathcal{M}_n
1194	      \;=\; \dim_\kappa C_{n+1} - \dim_\kappa K_n,
1195	  \]
1196	  which is the difference identity \(\mathrm{hdiff}\) consumed by
1197	  \cref{lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq}. No graded structure is used in proving the
1198	  underlying vector-space identity -- it is pure rank--nullity.
1199	\end{lemma}
1200	
1201	\begin{proof}
1202	  \uses{lem:finrank_range_add_finrank_ker_mathlib,
1203	    lem:finrank_ses_additive_mathlib}
1204	  By rank--nullity (\cref{lem:finrank_range_add_finrank_ker_mathlib}),
1205	  \(\dim_\kappa V = \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi) + \dim_\kappa(\operatorname{range}
1206	  \varphi)\), so \(\dim_\kappa(\operatorname{range}\varphi) = \dim_\kappa V -
1207	  \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi)\). By additivity on the short exact sequence
1208	  \(0 \to \operatorname{range}\varphi \to W \to W/\operatorname{range}\varphi \to 0\)
1209	  (\cref{lem:finrank_ses_additive_mathlib}),
1210	  \(\dim_\kappa(W/\operatorname{range}\varphi) = \dim_\kappa W -
1211	  \dim_\kappa(\operatorname{range}\varphi) = \dim_\kappa W - \dim_\kappa V +
1212	  \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi)\). Rearranging gives
1213	  \(\dim_\kappa W - \dim_\kappa V = \dim_\kappa(W/\operatorname{range}\varphi) -
1214	  \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi)\). The degreewise application identifies
1215	  \(\ker(x : \mathcal{M}_n \to \mathcal{M}_{n+1})\) and
1216	  \(\mathcal{M}_{n+1}/\operatorname{range}(x : \mathcal{M}_n \to \mathcal{M}_{n+1})\)
1217	  with the degree-\(n\) piece of the kernel subquotient and the degree-\((n+1)\)
1218	  piece of the cokernel subquotient (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff}),
1219	  the latter because the degree-\((n+1)\) graded piece of the homogeneous submodule
1220	  \(x\mathcal{M}\) is exactly \(x \mathcal{M}_n\)
1221	  (\cref{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}).
1222	\end{proof}
1223	
1224	\subsubsection*{The ambient subquotient induction}
1225	
1226	\begin{lemma}[Rationality of the ambient subquotient Hilbert function]
1227	  \label{lem:graded_subquotient_isRatHilb}
1228	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.subquotient_hilbertSeries_rational}
1229	  \uses{def:graded_subquotientHilb, lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker,
1230	    lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff,
1231	    lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer, lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero,
1232	    lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq, lem:ratHilb_bump}
1233	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Commutative Algebra", \S "Noetherian graded
1234	  % rings", Proposition \texttt{proposition-graded-hilbert-polynomial} (Tag
1235	  % 00K1), proof (read from references/hilbert-serre-algebra.tex, L13908-L13914).
1236	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag 00K1 (induction on the number of
1237	  degree-one generators).}
1238	  Let \((N, N')\) be a subquotient datum of length \(r\)
1239	  (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb}) with ambient Hilbert function
1240	  \(\mathrm{hilb}\). Then \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(\mathrm{hilb}, r)\)
1241	  (\cref{def:ratHilb}): the difference \(n \mapsto \dim_\kappa(N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)
1242	  - \dim_\kappa(N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\) is, for \(n \gg 0\), the coefficient
1243	  sequence of a rational series \(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-r}\) with \(p \in
1244	  \mathbb{Q}[X]\). The induction is on the length \(r\), and every object that
1245	  appears is an ambient pair of homogeneous submodules of the fixed graded module
1246	  \(M\).
1247	\end{lemma}
1248	
1249	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: [Stacks Project], Tag 00K1, proof, induction set-up (read
1250	% from references/hilbert-serre-algebra.tex, L13908-L13914).
1251	% "We prove this by induction on the minimal number of
1252	%  generators of $S_1$. If this number is $0$, then
1253	%  $M_n = 0$ for all $n \gg 0$ and the result holds.
1254	%  To prove the induction step, let $x\in S_1$
1255	%  be one of a minimal set of generators, such that
1256	%  the induction hypothesis applies to the
1257	%  graded ring $S/(x)$."
1258	\begin{proof}
1259	  \uses{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker,
1260	    lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff,
1261	    lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer, lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero,
1262	    lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq, lem:ratHilb_bump}
1263	  We induct on the length \(r\).
1264	
1265	  \emph{Base case \(r = 0\).} With no endomorphisms, the finiteness witness says
1266	  \(M\) is a finite module over \(\kappa[\,] = \kappa\), so \(M\) is a
1267	  finite-dimensional \(\kappa\)-vector space. Hence \(N \cap \mathcal{M}_n = 0\) and
1268	  \(N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n = 0\) for all \(n \gg 0\), so \(\mathrm{hilb}(n) = 0\) for
1269	  \(n \gg 0\). By \cref{lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero} an eventually-zero function is
1270	  rational of order \(0\), giving \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(\mathrm{hilb}, 0)\).
1271	
1272	  \emph{Inductive step \(r \ge 1\).} Set \(x = t_{r-1}\). By the kernel/cokernel
1273	  closure (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker}) the kernel subquotient
1274	  \(K = (N \cap x^{-1}(N'),\, N')\) and the cokernel subquotient
1275	  \(C = (N,\, N' + x \cdot N)\) are subquotient data of length \(r-1\), with valid
1276	  finiteness witnesses supplied by the finiteness transfer
1277	  (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer}). The induction hypothesis applied
1278	  to \(K\) and \(C\) gives \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_K, r-1)\) and
1279	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_C, r-1)\) (\cref{def:ratHilb}). By the degreewise
1280	  difference identity (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff}),
1281	  \[
1282	    \mathrm{hilb}(n+1) - \mathrm{hilb}(n) \;=\; h_C(n+1) - h_K(n)
1283	    \qquad \text{for all } n .
1284	  \]
1285	  Feeding \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_C, r-1)\), \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_K, r-1)\) and this
1286	  difference identity into the inductive-step engine
1287	  (\cref{lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq}) yields \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(\mathrm{hilb}, r)\). (The
1288	  common order \(r-1\) for \(h_C\) and \(h_K\) is arranged, if necessary, by raising
1289	  the lower one with \cref{lem:ratHilb_bump}.) This completes the induction.
1290	\end{proof}

1292	\section{Support and freeness predicates}
1293	\label{sec:quot_predicates}
1294	
1295	The Quot functor (\cref{def:quot_functor}) and the Grassmannian
1296	(\cref{def:grassmannian_scheme}) classify quotient sheaves subject to two
1297	geometric conditions: a coherent quotient must have \emph{schematic support
1298	proper over the base}, and the Grassmannian's quotients must be \emph{locally
1299	free of a fixed rank}. This section introduces both predicates.
1300	
1301	\begin{definition}
1302	
1303	\leanok
1304	[Annihilator ideal sheaf of a sheaf of modules]
1305	  \label{def:modules_annihilator}
1306	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.annihilator}
1307	  \uses{lem:annihilator_localization_eq_map, lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}
1308	  % NOTE: The definition itself is formalized and axiom-clean; the inclusion direction
1309	  % \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})(U) \subseteq \mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}(\mathcal{F}(U))\)
1310	  % (\cref{lem:modules_annihilator_ideal_le}) is closed. The reverse inclusion, which identifies
1311	  % the annihilator sheaf with the module-annihilator on affine opens, remains bridge-gated on
1312	  % \cref{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}.
1313	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
1314	  % Polynomials" (read from
1315	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L468-L471).
1316	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1317	  % "Let $X\to S$ be a finite type morphism of noetherian schemes,
1318	  %  and let $L$ be a line bundle on $X$. Let $\F$
1319	  %  be any coherent sheaf on
1320	  %  $X$ whose schematic support is proper over $S$."
1321	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
1322	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a sheaf of
1323	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules that is \emph{quasi-coherent and of finite type}
1324	  (over a locally noetherian base, so that on each affine open \(U\) the section
1325	  module \(\mathcal{F}(U)\) is a finitely generated \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\)-module).
1326	  The \emph{annihilator ideal sheaf} of \(\mathcal{F}\) is the ideal sheaf
1327	  \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})\) defined on affine opens: for each affine open
1328	  \(U \subseteq X\),
1329	  \[
1330	    \mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})(U)
1331	    \;=\; \mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}\bigl(\mathcal{F}(U)\bigr)
1332	    \;\subseteq\; \mathcal{O}_X(U),
1333	  \]
1334	  the annihilator of the \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\)-module \(\mathcal{F}(U)\). These
1335	  local data patch to a coherent ideal sheaf on \(X\) whose vanishing locus is
1336	  the set-theoretic support of \(\mathcal{F}\).
1337	
1338	  \emph{Well-definedness on basic opens.} The coherence condition for an ideal
1339	  sheaf requires that, for an affine open \(U\) and \(f \in \mathcal{O}_X(U)\),
1340	  the section \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})(U)\) restricts correctly to the basic
1341	  open \(D(f) \subseteq U\), i.e. that
1342	  \(\mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(D(f))}(\mathcal{F}(D(f)))\) is the extension of
1343	  \(\mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}(\mathcal{F}(U))\) along the localization map
1344	  \(\mathcal{O}_X(U) \to \mathcal{O}_X(D(f))\). This is exactly where the two
1345	  hypotheses enter. The bridge
1346	  \cref{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen} supplies, for a quasi-coherent
1347	  finite-type \(\mathcal{F}\), that \(\mathcal{O}_X(D(f)) = (\mathcal{O}_X(U))_f\)
1348	  and that the restriction \(\mathcal{F}(U) \to \mathcal{F}(D(f))\) exhibits
1349	  \(\mathcal{F}(D(f))\) as the localized module \((\mathrm{powers}\,f)^{-1}
1350	  \mathcal{F}(U)\); the algebra engine
1351	  \cref{lem:annihilator_localization_eq_map} then yields, using that
1352	  \(\mathcal{F}(U)\) is finitely generated, the required identity
1353	  \(\mathrm{Ann}_{(\mathcal{O}_X(U))_f}\bigl((\mathrm{powers}\,f)^{-1}
1354	  \mathcal{F}(U)\bigr) = \mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}(\mathcal{F}(U)) \cdot
1355	  (\mathcal{O}_X(U))_f\). Thus the local annihilators are compatible with basic-open
1356	  restriction, which is the patching datum that makes \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})\)
1357	  a well-defined ideal sheaf. The finite-type hypothesis is genuinely required:
1358	  without finite generation of \(\mathcal{F}(U)\) the annihilator need not commute
1359	  with localization, and the local data fail to patch.
1360	
1361	  \emph{Note.} Mathlib does not carry an annihilator for sheaves of modules on a scheme.
1362	\end{definition}
1363	
1364	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1365	[Annihilator ideal bounded by the module annihilator on affine opens]
1366	  \label{lem:modules_annihilator_ideal_le}
1367	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.annihilator_ideal_le}
1368	  \uses{def:modules_annihilator}
1369	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, \(\mathcal{F}\) a sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules and
1370	  \(U \subseteq X\) an affine open. Then the section over \(U\) of the annihilator
1371	  ideal sheaf \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})\) (\cref{def:modules_annihilator}) is
1372	  contained in the module-annihilator of the sections of \(\mathcal{F}\) over
1373	  \(U\):
1374	  \[
1375	    \mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})(U)
1376	    \;\subseteq\; \mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}\bigl(\mathcal{F}(U)\bigr).
1377	  \]
1378	  This is the inclusion direction available directly from the definition of
1379	  \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})\); the reverse inclusion is the basic-open coherence
1380	  statement gated on \cref{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}. Proved directly
1381	  in Lean. It supports the well-definedness of the schematic support
1382	  (\cref{def:schematic_support}).
1383	\end{lemma}
1384	
1385	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1386	[Annihilator of a finitely generated module commutes with
1387	  localization]
1388	  \label{lem:annihilator_localization_eq_map}
1389	  \lean{Module.annihilator_isLocalizedModule_eq_map}
1390	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, let \(S \subseteq R\) be a multiplicative
1391	  submonoid, and let \(R_S = S^{-1}R\) be the localization of \(R\) at \(S\). Let
1392	  \(M\) be a \emph{finitely generated} \(R\)-module and let \(f : M \to M_S\) be
1393	  an \(R\)-linear map exhibiting \(M_S\) as the localized module \(S^{-1}M\)
1394	  (i.e.\ \(f\) is a localization of \(M\) at \(S\)). Then the annihilator of
1395	  \(M_S\) over \(R_S\) is the extension of the annihilator of \(M\) along the
1396	  structure map \(R \to R_S\):
1397	  \[
1398	    \mathrm{Ann}_{R_S}(M_S)
1399	    \;=\; (\mathrm{Ann}_R M)\cdot R_S
1400	    \;=\; \mathrm{map}\bigl(R \to R_S\bigr)\bigl(\mathrm{Ann}_R M\bigr).
1401	  \]
1402	  Equivalently, for finitely generated \(M\) the annihilator commutes with
1403	  localization: \(\mathrm{Ann}(S^{-1}M) = S^{-1}\,\mathrm{Ann}(M)\).
1404	
1405	  \emph{The finite-generation hypothesis is essential.} For a module that is not
1406	  finitely generated the inclusion \(S^{-1}\,\mathrm{Ann}(M) \subseteq
1407	  \mathrm{Ann}(S^{-1}M)\) can be strict, so the identity fails. This is the
1408	  standard commutative-algebra fact that the annihilator of a finite module
1409	  localizes; it is recorded here as the load-bearing algebra engine for the
1410	  basic-open coherence of \cref{def:modules_annihilator}.
1411	\end{lemma}
1412	
1413	\begin{proof}
1414	  \(\supseteq\): if \(r \in \mathrm{Ann}_R M\), then \(r\) kills every element of
1415	  \(M\), hence its image \(r/1 \in R_S\) kills every element of \(M_S\) (each such
1416	  element is \(m/s = (1/s)\cdot f(m)\) for some \(m \in M\), \(s \in S\), and
1417	  \((r/1)\cdot(m/s) = (1/s)\cdot f(r\,m) = 0\)). Thus
1418	  \((\mathrm{Ann}_R M)\cdot R_S \subseteq \mathrm{Ann}_{R_S}(M_S)\).
1419	
1420	  \(\subseteq\): write \(M = \langle m_1, \dots, m_n\rangle\) using the
1421	  finite-generation hypothesis, and let \(y \in \mathrm{Ann}_{R_S}(M_S)\). Write
1422	  \(y = a/s\) with \(a \in R\), \(s \in S\). For each generator \(m_i\) the element
1423	  \(y\cdot f(m_i) = (a/s)\cdot f(m_i) = 0\) in \(M_S\); by the definition of the
1424	  localized module this means there is \(u_i \in S\) with \(u_i\,a\,m_i = 0\) in
1425	  \(M\). Put \(u = \prod_{i=1}^n u_i \in S\). Then \(u\,a\) kills every generator
1426	  \(m_i\), hence kills all of \(M\), so \(u\,a \in \mathrm{Ann}_R M\). Finally
1427	  \(y = a/s = (u\,a)/(u\,s)\) lies in \(S^{-1}\,\mathrm{Ann}_R M =
1428	  (\mathrm{Ann}_R M)\cdot R_S\). Therefore \(\mathrm{Ann}_{R_S}(M_S) \subseteq
1429	  (\mathrm{Ann}_R M)\cdot R_S\), and combined with the reverse inclusion the two
1430	  ideals coincide. The use of a single common multiplier \(u = \prod_i u_i\) is
1431	  exactly the step that requires finitely many generators.
1432	\end{proof}
1433	
1434	\begin{lemma}[Sections on a basic open localize the affine ring]
1435	  \label{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}
1436	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsAffineOpen.isLocalization_basicOpen}
1437	  \mathlibok
1438	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
1439	  (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.AffineScheme}).}
1440	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, \(U \subseteq X\) an affine open, and
1441	  \(f \in \Gamma(X, U)\). Then the restriction map
1442	  \(\Gamma(X, U) \to \Gamma\bigl(X,\, D(f)\bigr)\) on the basic open
1443	  \(D(f) = X.\mathrm{basicOpen}\,f\) exhibits \(\Gamma(X, D(f))\) as the
1444	  localization of \(\Gamma(X, U)\) away from \(f\); that is, it is
1445	  \(\mathrm{IsLocalization.Away}\,f\), so
1446	  \(\Gamma(X, D(f)) = (\Gamma(X, U))_f\) as a \(\Gamma(X,U)\)-algebra.
1447	\end{lemma}
1448	
1449	\begin{lemma}[Quasi-coherent sections on a basic open are the localized module]
1450	  \label{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}
1451	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.isLocalizedModule_basicOpen}
1452	  \uses{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}
1453	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Schemes" (chapter tag 020J), \S 7
1454	  % "Quasi-coherent sheaves on affines", Lemma \texttt{lemma-spec-sheaves},
1455	  % item (4) (read from references/stacks-schemes.tex, L691-L702).
1456	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1457	  % "Let $R$ be a ring. Let $M$ be an $R$-module. Let $\widetilde M$
1458	  %  be the sheaf of $\mathcal{O}_{\Spec(R)}$-modules
1459	  %  associated to $M$.
1460	  %  [...]
1461	  %  \item For every $f\in R$ we have $\Gamma(D(f), \widetilde M) = M_f$
1462	  %  as an $R_f$-module."
1463	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Schemes", \S 7, Lemma on the sheaves
1464	  \(\widetilde M\) on \(\Spec(R)\), item (4).}
1465	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let \(\mathcal{M}\) be a \emph{quasi-coherent} sheaf
1466	  of modules on \(X\). Let \(U \subseteq X\) be an
1467	  affine open and \(f \in \Gamma(X, U)\). Then:
1468	  \begin{enumerate}
1469	    \item \(\Gamma\bigl(X, D(f)\bigr)\) is the localization \((\Gamma(X,U))_f\)
1470	      of \(\Gamma(X,U)\) away from \(f\)
1471	      (\cref{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}); and
1472	    \item the section-restriction map
1473	      \(\mathcal{M}(U) \to \mathcal{M}\bigl(D(f)\bigr)\) exhibits
1474	      \(\mathcal{M}(D(f))\) as the localized module
1475	      \((\mathrm{powers}\,f)^{-1}\,\mathcal{M}(U)\); that is, it is a localization
1476	      of the \(\Gamma(X,U)\)-module \(\mathcal{M}(U)\) at the submonoid
1477	      \(\mathrm{powers}\,f = \{1, f, f^2, \dots\}\).
1478	  \end{enumerate}
1479	\end{lemma}
1480	
1481	\begin{proof}
1482	  \uses{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}
1483	  Part (1) is \cref{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}, the Mathlib statement
1484	  that on an affine open \(U\) the sections over the basic open \(D(f)\) form the
1485	  localization of \(\Gamma(X,U)\) away from \(f\).
1486	
1487	  For part (2), identify the affine open \(U\) with \(\Spec \Gamma(X, U)\). Over
1488	  this affine, a quasi-coherent sheaf is \(\widetilde{N}\) for the
1489	  \(\Gamma(X,U)\)-module \(N = \mathcal{M}(U)\), the sheaf associated to
1490	  \(N\) (no finiteness of \(N\) is required for this step). The Spec-local
1491	  computation of the sections of \(\widetilde{N}\) on a
1492	  basic open gives \(\Gamma\bigl(D(f), \widetilde{N}\bigr) = N_f\) as an
1493	  \((\Gamma(X,U))_f\)-module, and the restriction map
1494	  \(\Gamma(\Spec \Gamma(X,U), \widetilde{N}) = N \to N_f\) is precisely the
1495	  localization homomorphism \(N \to (\mathrm{powers}\,f)^{-1} N\). Transporting
1496	  this identification across the affine isomorphism
1497	  \(U \cong \Spec \Gamma(X, U)\) -- under which \(\mathcal{M}|_U\) corresponds to
1498	  \(\widetilde{N}\) by quasi-coherence and the basic open \(D(f) \subseteq U\)
1499	  corresponds to the standard open \(D(f) \subseteq \Spec\Gamma(X,U)\) -- shows
1500	  that the restriction \(\mathcal{M}(U) \to \mathcal{M}(D(f))\) is the
1501	  localization of \(N = \mathcal{M}(U)\) at \(\mathrm{powers}\,f\), i.e.\ it is a
1502	  localized module at \(\mathrm{powers}\,f\). The substantive content is this
1503	  transport of the Spec-local localization fact for the sheaf \(\widetilde{N}\)
1504	  to a general quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{M}\) via the affine identification.
1505	\end{proof}

1507	\begin{definition}
1508	
1509	\leanok
1510	[Schematic support of a sheaf of modules]
1511	  \label{def:schematic_support}
1512	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.schematicSupport}
1513	  \uses{def:modules_annihilator, lem:modules_annihilator_ideal_le,
1514	    def:schematic_support_immersion}
1515	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
1516	  % Polynomials" (read from
1517	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L468-L471).
1518	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1519	  % "Let $X\to S$ be a finite type morphism of noetherian schemes,
1520	  %  and let $L$ be a line bundle on $X$. Let $\F$
1521	  %  be any coherent sheaf on
1522	  %  $X$ whose schematic support is proper over $S$."
1523	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
1524	  Let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a sheaf of modules on a scheme \(X\). The
1525	  \emph{schematic support} of \(\mathcal{F}\) is the closed subscheme of \(X\)
1526	  cut out by its annihilator ideal sheaf (\cref{def:modules_annihilator}):
1527	  \[
1528	    \mathrm{schematicSupport}(\mathcal{F})
1529	    \;=\; V\bigl(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})\bigr) \;\hookrightarrow\; X,
1530	  \]
1531	  equipped with its canonical closed immersion into \(X\). This realises
1532	  Nitsure's ``schematic support of \(\mathcal{F}\)'' as a closed subscheme of
1533	  \(X\).
1534	\end{definition}
1535	
1536	\begin{definition}\leanok
1537	[Schematic-support immersion]
1538	  \label{def:schematic_support_immersion}
1539	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.schematicSupportι}
1540	  \uses{def:schematic_support, def:modules_annihilator}
1541	  Let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a sheaf of modules on a scheme \(X\). The
1542	  \emph{schematic-support immersion} is the canonical closed immersion
1543	  \[
1544	    \mathrm{schematicSupport}(\mathcal{F}) \;\hookrightarrow\; X
1545	  \]
1546	  of the schematic support (\cref{def:schematic_support}) into the ambient
1547	  scheme, namely the subscheme immersion of the annihilator ideal sheaf
1548	  \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})\) (\cref{def:modules_annihilator}). It is a
1549	  quasi-compact preimmersion onto the support, and it is the morphism whose
1550	  composite with a base morphism \(f : X \to S\) defines proper support
1551	  (\cref{def:has_proper_support}). Proved directly in Lean.
1552	\end{definition}

1554	\begin{lemma}[Properness as a morphism property]
1555	  \label{lem:isProper_mathlib}
1556	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsProper}
1557	  \mathlibok
1558	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
1559	  (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Morphisms.Proper}).}
1560	  A morphism \(f : X \to S\) of schemes is \emph{proper} iff it is separated,
1561	  universally closed, and locally of finite type. Properness is stable under base
1562	  change: if \(f : X \to S\) is proper and \(g : T \to S\) is any morphism then
1563	  the base change \(X \times_S T \to T\) is proper. Consequently the base-change
1564	  clause that Nitsure invokes (``properness \ldots\ preserved by base-change'')
1565	  applies directly to the proper-support condition below.
1566	\end{lemma}
1567	
1568	\begin{definition}
1569	
1570	\leanok
1571	[Proper support over the base]
1572	  \label{def:has_proper_support}
1573	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.HasProperSupport}
1574	  \uses{def:schematic_support, def:schematic_support_immersion, lem:isProper_mathlib}
1575	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "The Functors $\Hilb_{X/S}$ and
1576	  % $\Quot_{E/X/S}$" (read from
1577	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L407-L409
1578	  % + L418-L419).
1579	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1580	  % "(i) a coherent sheaf $\F$ on $X_T = X\times_ST$
1581	  %  such that the schematic support of $\F$ is proper over $T$
1582	  %  and $\F$ is flat over $T$, together with
1583	  %  [...]
1584	  %  Then as properness and flatness are preserved
1585	  %  by base-change, and as tensor-product is right exact,"
1586	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
1587	  Let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a sheaf of modules on a scheme \(X\) and let
1588	  \(f : X \to S\) be a morphism. We say \(\mathcal{F}\) \emph{has proper support
1589	  over \(S\) (along \(f\))} when the composite morphism
1590	  \[
1591	    \mathrm{schematicSupport}(\mathcal{F}) \hookrightarrow X \xrightarrow{f} S
1592	  \]
1593	  is proper (\cref{lem:isProper_mathlib}). Since properness is stable under base
1594	  change (\cref{lem:isProper_mathlib}), this condition is preserved by pull-back
1595	  along any \(S'\to S\), which is exactly what the well-definedness of the Quot
1596	  functor's pullback action requires.
1597	\end{definition}

1599	\begin{definition}
1600	
1601	\leanok
1602	[Locally free of rank \(d\)]
1603	  \label{def:is_locally_free_of_rank}

1613	  %  the set of all equivalence classes $\langle \F, q\rangle$ of quotients

1625	\section{The Quot functor}
1626	\label{sec:quot_functor}
1627	
1628	\begin{definition}\leanok
1629	[Quot functor]
1630	  \label{def:quot_functor}
1631	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.QuotFunctor}
1632	  \uses{def:hilbert_polynomial, def:has_proper_support}
1633	
1634	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "The Functors \(\Hilb_{X/S}\) and
1635	  % \(\Quot_{E/X/S}\)" (read from
1636	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L394-L433
1637	  % + L481-L498).
1638	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1639	  % "Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme and let \(X\to S\) be a finite type
1640	  %  scheme over it. Let \(E\) be a coherent sheaf on \(X\).
1641	  %  Let \(Sch_S\) denote the category of all locally noetherian schemes
1642	  %  over \(S\). For any \(T\to S\) in \(Sch_S\), a \textbf{family of quotients of \(E\)
1643	  %  parametrised by \(T\)} will mean
1644	  %  a pair \((\F,q)\) consisting of
1645	  %
1646	  %  (i) a coherent sheaf \(\F\) on \(X_T = X\times_ST\)
1647	  %  such that the schematic support of \(\F\) is proper over \(T\)
1648	  %  and \(\F\) is flat over \(T\), together with
1649	  %
1650	  %  (ii) a surjective \({\mathcal O}_{X_T}\)-linear homomorphism of sheaves
1651	  %  \(q:E_T  \to \F\) where \(E_T\) is the pull-back of \(E\) under
1652	  %  the projection \(X_T \to X\).
1653	  %
1654	  %  Two such families \((\F,q)\) and \((\F,q)\) parametrised by \(T\) will
1655	  %  be regarded as equivalent if \(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\)),
1656	  %  and \(\langle \F, q \rangle\) will denote
1657	  %  an equivalence class. Then as properness and flatness are preserved
1658	  %  by base-change, and as tensor-product is right exact,
1659	  %  the pull-back of \(\langle \F, q \rangle\) under an \(S\)-morphism \(T'\to T\) is
1660	  %  well-defined, which gives
1661	  %  a set-valued contravariant functor
1662	  %  \(\Quot_{E/X/S} : Sch_S \to Sets\) under which
1663	  %  \(\)T\mapsto  \{\mbox{ All } \langle \F, q \rangle \mbox{ parametrised by }T\}\(\)
1664	  %  [...]
1665	  %  This shows that the functor \(\Quot_{E/X/S}\) naturally decomposes
1666	  %  as a co-product
1667	  %  \(\)\Quot_{E/X/S} = \coprod_{{\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]}\, \Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}\(\)
1668	  %  where for any polynomial \({\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]\), the functor
1669	  %  \(\Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}\) associates
1670	  %  to any \(T\) the set of all equivalence classes of
1671	  %  families \(\langle \F,q \rangle\) such that at each \(t\in T\) the
1672	  %  Hilbert polynomial of the restriction \(\F_t\), calculated
1673	  %  using the pull-back of \(L\), is \({\Phi}\)."
1674	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
1675	  Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, \(\pi : X \to S\) a finite-type morphism,
1676	  \(L\) a line bundle on \(X\), \(E\) a coherent sheaf on \(X\), and
1677	  \(\Phi \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\). The \emph{Quot functor with Hilbert
1678	  polynomial \(\Phi\)}
1679	  \[
1680	    \Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S} : (\mathrm{Sch}/S)^{op} \longrightarrow \mathbf{Set}
1681	  \]
1682	  sends an \(S\)-scheme \(T \to S\) to the set of equivalence classes
1683	  \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle\) of pairs \((\mathcal{F}, q)\) where
1684	  \begin{enumerate}
1685	    \item \(\mathcal{F}\) is a coherent sheaf on \(X_T = X \times_S T\) whose
1686	      schematic support is proper over \(T\) (\cref{def:has_proper_support}) and
1687	      which is flat over \(T\);
1688	    \item \(q : E_T \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) is a surjective
1689	      \(\mathcal{O}_{X_T}\)-linear homomorphism, where \(E_T\) is the pull-back
1690	      of \(E\) along \(X_T \to X\);
1691	    \item for every \(t \in T\) the Hilbert polynomial of \(\mathcal{F}|_{X_t}\)
1692	      with respect to \(L|_{X_t}\) (\cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}) equals
1693	      \(\Phi\).
1694	  \end{enumerate}
1695	  Two pairs \((\mathcal{F}, q)\) and \((\mathcal{F}', q')\) are equivalent iff
1696	  \(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\). On morphisms \(f : T' \to T\) over \(S\), the functor
1697	  acts by pulling back the quotient along \(X_{T'} \to X_T\) (well-defined
1698	  since tensor product is right-exact and preserves both flatness and
1699	  properness). The Hilbert scheme is the special case
1700	  \(\Hilb^{\Phi,L}_{X/S} := \Quot^{\Phi,L}_{\mathcal{O}_X/X/S}\).
1701	\end{definition}

1703	\section{The Grassmannian scheme}
1704	\label{sec:grassmannian_scheme}
1705	
1706	The Quot construction proceeds by embedding \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\) as a
1707	locally closed subfunctor of a Grassmannian over \(S\). Since Mathlib carries
1708	no Grassmannian \emph{scheme} (only a finite-rank-quotient variety in
1709	linear algebra), we package the construction here.
1710	
1711	\begin{definition}\leanok
1712	[Grassmannian scheme]
1713	  \label{def:grassmannian_scheme}
1714	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Grassmannian}
1715	  \uses{def:quot_functor, def:is_locally_free_of_rank}
1716	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, "Elementary Examples, Exercises~(2)" and
1717	  % "Construction of Grassmannian" (read from
1718	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L546-L599
1719	  % + L773-L850).
1720	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1721	  % "For any integers \(r\ge d\ge 1\), an explicit construction
1722	  %  the Grassmannian scheme
1723	  %  \(\grass(r,d)\) over \(\Z\), together with the tautological
1724	  %  quotient \(u:\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\grass(r,d)} \to \U\) where \
1725	  %  \(\U\) is a rank \(d\) locally free sheaf on \(\grass(r,d)\),
1726	  %  has been given at the end of this section. [...]
1727	  %  Show that \(\grass(r,d)\) together with the quotient
1728	  %  \(u:\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\grass(r,d)} \to \U\) represents the
1729	  %  contravariant functor
1730	  %  \(\)\Grass(r,d) = \Quot_{\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\Z}/\Z/\Z}^{d, {\mathcal O}_{\Z}}\(\)
1731	  %  from schemes to sets, which associates to any \(T\) the
1732	  %  set of all equivalence classes
1733	  %  \(\langle \F, q\rangle\) of quotients \(q: \oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_T \to \F\)
1734	  %  where \(\F\) is a locally free sheaf on \(T\) of rank \(d\).
1735	  %  [...]
1736	  %  Using the above show that, more generally, if \(S\) is a scheme and
1737	  %  \(E\) is a locally free \({\mathcal O}_S\)-module of rank \(r\),
1738	  %  the functor \(\Grass(E,d)=\Quot_{E/S/S}^{d,{\mathcal O}_S}\)
1739	  %  on all \(S\)-schemes which
1740	  %  by definition associates to any \(T\)
1741	  %  the set of all equivalence classes \(\langle \F, q\rangle\) of quotients
1742	  %  \(q: E_T \to \F\) where \(\F\) is a locally free sheaf on \(T\) of rank \(d\),
1743	  %  is representable. The representing scheme
1744	  %  is denoted by \(\grass(E,d)\) and is called the rank \(d\)
1745	  %  relative Grassmannian of \(E\) over \(S\)."
1746	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1, Exercise (2) and "Construction of
1747	  Grassmannian" (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
1748	  Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme and let \(V\) be a locally free
1749	  \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-module of rank \(r\). For an integer \(1 \le d \le r\), the
1750	  \emph{Grassmannian of rank-\(d\) quotients of \(V\)} is the functor
1751	  \[
1752	    \mathrm{Grass}(V, d) : (\mathrm{Sch}/S)^{op} \longrightarrow \mathbf{Set},
1753	    \qquad
1754	    T \longmapsto \{ \langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle :
1755	    q : V_T \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F},\; \mathcal{F}
1756	    \text{ locally free of rank } d \}
1757	  \]
1758	  with equivalence \(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\); the rank-\(d\) local freeness of
1759	  \(\mathcal{F}\) is as in \cref{def:is_locally_free_of_rank}. Concretely,
1760	  \(\mathrm{Grass}(V, d) = \Quot^{d,\mathcal{O}_S}_{V/S/S}\): the Quot functor
1761	  (\cref{def:quot_functor}) in the special case \(X = S\), \(E = V\), constant
1762	  Hilbert polynomial \(\Phi = d\).
1763	\end{definition}

1803	  % "For any integers \(r\ge d\ge 1\), the Grassmannian scheme

1805	  %  quotient \(u:\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\grass(r,d)} \to \U\) where \
1806	  %  \(\U\) is a rank \(d\) locally free sheaf on \(\grass(r,d)\),
1807	  %  can be explicitly constructed as follows. [...]
1808	  %  the schemes
1809	  %  \(U^I\), as \(I\) varies over all the
1810	  %  \({r\choose d}\) different
1811	  %  subsets of  \(\{ 1,\ldots, r\}\) of cardinality \(d\),
1812	  %  can be glued together by the co-cycle \((\theta_{I,J})\) to form a
1813	  %  finite-type scheme \(\grass(r,d)\) over \(\Z\). As each \(U^I\) is
1814	  %  isomorphic to \(\AA^{d(r-d)}_{\Z}\), it follows that
1815	  %  \(\grass(r,d) \to \spec \Z\) is smooth of relative dimension \(d(r-d)\).
1816	  %  [...]
1817	  %  We now show that \(\pi : \grass(r,d) \to \spec \Z\) is proper. It is enough to
1818	  %  verify the valuative criterion of properness for discrete valuation rings.
1819	  %  [...]
1820	  %  As \(\U\) is given by the transition functions \(g_{I,J}\) described above,
1821	  %  the determinant line bundle
1822	  %  \(\det(\U)\) is given by the transition functions
1823	  %  \(\det(g_{I,J}) = 1/P^I_J \in GL_1(U^I_J)\).
1824	  %  [...]
1825	  %  We leave it to the reader to verify that the sections \(\sigma_I\) form
1826	  %  a linear system which is base point free and separates points
1827	  %  relative to \(\spec \Z\), and so gives an embedding of
1828	  %  \(\grass(r,d)\) into \(\PP^m_{\Z}\) where \(m = {r\choose d}-1\).
1829	  %  This is a closed embedding by the properness of
1830	  %  \(\pi:\grass(r,d) \to \spec \Z\). In particular, \(\det(\U)\)
1831	  %  is a relatively very ample line bundle on \(\grass(r,d)\) over \(\Z\)."
1832	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1, "Construction of Grassmannian" (FGA
1833	  Explained Ch.~5).}
1834	  Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, \(V\) a locally free \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-module
1835	  of rank \(r\), and \(1 \le d \le r\). The functor \(\mathrm{Grass}(V,d)\) of
1836	  \cref{def:grassmannian_scheme} is representable by a smooth projective
1837	  \(S\)-scheme \(\mathrm{Gr}_S(V, d) \to S\) of relative dimension \(d(r-d)\),
1838	  equipped with a tautological quotient
1839	  \(\pi^* V \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{U}\) of rank \(d\). The determinant
1840	  line bundle \(\det(\mathcal{U})\) is relatively very ample, and the
1841	  associated linear system gives a closed embedding
1842	  \(\mathrm{Gr}_S(V, d) \hookrightarrow
1843	  \mathbb{P}_S\!\left(\bigwedge^d V\right)\) -- the \emph{Pl\"ucker embedding}.
1844	
1845	\end{theorem}
1846	
1847	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the "Construction of Grassmannian" sub-section of
1848	% [Nitsure], \S 1 (references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex,
1849	% L773-L930), which gives the construction in five named steps
1850	% ("Construction by gluing together affine patches", "Separatedness",
1851	% "Properness", "Universal quotient", "Projective embedding"). The total
1852	% verbatim text is ~150 lines; it is reproduced piecewise in the
1853	% SOURCE QUOTE comment of the theorem statement above.
1854	\begin{proof}
1855	  
1856	  Over \(S = \Spec \mathbb{Z}\) and \(V = \mathcal{O}_S^{\oplus r}\) the
1857	  Grassmannian \(\mathrm{Gr}(r, d)\) is constructed by gluing
1858	  \(\binom{r}{d}\) affine charts \(U^I = \Spec\mathbb{Z}[X^I]\) indexed by
1859	  size-\(d\) subsets \(I \subseteq \{1, \dots, r\}\). The chart \(U^I\)
1860	  carries the matrix \(X^I\) of size \(d \times r\) whose \(I\)-th \(d \times d\)
1861	  minor is the identity, with the remaining \(d(r-d)\) entries free
1862	  variables (so \(U^I \cong \mathbb{A}^{d(r-d)}_{\mathbb{Z}}\)). The
1863	  transition maps \(\theta_{I,J} : U^I \cap U^J \to U^J \cap U^I\) defined
1864	  by \(X^J \mapsto (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\) on the locus where \(\det X^I_J\) is
1865	  invertible satisfy the cocycle condition
1866	  \(\theta_{I,K} = \theta_{I,J} \circ \theta_{J,K}\). The glued
1867	  \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec\mathbb{Z}\) is smooth of relative dimension
1868	  \(d(r-d)\). Separatedness is checked from the diagonal
1869	  \(\Delta_{I,J} \subset U^I \times U^J\) cut out by \(X^J_I X^I - X^J = 0\).
1870	  Properness follows from the valuative criterion for DVRs: given
1871	  \(\varphi : \Spec K \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) over a DVR \(R\) with fraction
1872	  field \(K\), factoring through some chart \(U^I\) via a ring map
1873	  \(f : \mathbb{Z}[X^I] \to K\), choose \(J\) minimising the valuation
1874	  \(\nu(f(P^I_J))\) of the minor; on the chart \(U^J\) all entries have
1875	  non-negative valuation, so \(f\) extends uniquely to \(\Spec R\).
1876	
1877	  The universal quotient \(u : \mathcal{O}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)}^{\oplus r}
1878	  \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{U}\) is given on \(U^I\) by the matrix \(X^I\)
1879	  and glued by \(g_{I,J} = (X^I_J)^{-1} \in GL_d(U^I \cap U^J)\). The
1880	  determinant line bundle \(\det\mathcal{U}\) has transition functions
1881	  \(\det g_{I,J} = 1/P^I_J\); the global sections
1882	  \(\sigma_I \in \Gamma(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d), \det\mathcal{U})\) defined by
1883	  \(\sigma_I|_{U^J} = P^J_I\) form a base-point-free linear system separating
1884	  points over \(\Spec\mathbb{Z}\), giving an embedding into
1885	  \(\mathbb{P}^{\binom{r}{d}-1}_{\mathbb{Z}}\), which is closed by
1886	  properness. Representability of \(\mathrm{Grass}(r,d)\) is then verified
1887	  on the universal pair \((\mathrm{Gr}(r,d), u)\): a quotient
1888	  \(q : \mathcal{O}_T^{\oplus r} \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) of
1889	  rank \(d\) on \(T\) is everywhere locally trivialised; on the locus where
1890	  the columns indexed by \(I\) are a basis the matrix of \(q\) has the form
1891	  \(X^I\), defining the classifying morphism \(T \to U^I \subset
1892	  \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\). Base change from \(\mathbb{Z}\) to a general noetherian
1893	  base \(S\) with a vector bundle \(V\) is direct: trivialise \(V\) on an open
1894	  cover and glue. For non-trivial \(V\) the same construction produces
1895	  \(\mathrm{Gr}_S(V, d)\) with Pl\"ucker embedding into
1896	  \(\mathbb{P}_S(\bigwedge^d V)\).
1897	\end{proof}

1899	\section{Out of scope}
1900	\label{sec:quot_out_of_scope}
1901	
1902	This chapter packages \emph{only} the Quot-foundations layer: the Hilbert
1903	polynomial, the Quot functor, and the Grassmannian scheme with its
1904	representability. The following are explicitly out of scope here:
1905	\begin{itemize}
1906	  \item \textbf{Representability of the Quot scheme} -- the
1907	    Grothendieck--Altman--Kleiman existence theorem and its proof skeleton
1908	    (boundedness, Grassmannian embedding, flattening stratification,
1909	    valuative criterion) are not constructed in this chapter.
1910	  \item \textbf{Flat base change of cohomology} (Stacks 02KH) and its
1911	    associated prerequisites -- not constructed in this chapter.
1912	  \item \textbf{Hilbert scheme as a separate chapter}. The Hilbert scheme
1913	    \(\Hilb_{X/S}\) is the special case
1914	    \(\Hilb_{X/S} = \Quot_{\mathcal{O}_X/X/S}\) of the Quot functor and is
1915	    introduced inline (\cref{def:quot_functor}); no separate chapter.
1916	  \item \textbf{Snapper's Lemma} -- the polynomial-eventually property of
1917	    Euler characteristics, used in \cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}. Cited
1918	    via Nitsure \S 1 and (textbook reference) Hartshorne III.5.2;
1919	    Lean formalization is a prerequisite sub-task.
1920	  \item \textbf{Identification with \(\mathbb{P}^n\) and relative
1921	    Grassmannian variants} (Nitsure \S 1, Exercises (1)--(4)) --
1922	    optional applications, not consumed by Route A.
1923	  \item \textbf{Group-scheme structure on \(\Pic_{C/k}\)} (A.2.c) -- the
1924	    abelian-group-scheme refinement is downstream. Its blueprint home is the
1925	    FGA Picard-representability assembly (the chapter that bundles the relative
1926	    Picard functor, its representability, and the abelian-group-scheme
1927	    structure on \(\Pic_{C/k}\)); that assembly lives in the parent project
1928	    which consumes this Quot-foundations layer and is not part of the present
1929	    subproject.
1930	  \item \textbf{Identity-component \(\Pic^0\) and abelian-variety
1931	    structure} (A.3) -- a separate Route A sub-row, fed by but not
1932	    constructed in this chapter.
1933	\end{itemize}

14	\section{Setup and motivation}
15	\label{sec:relspec_setup}
16	
17	The Route~A construction of \(J = \Pic^0_{C/k}\) as a \(g\)-dimensional abelian variety is
18	organised around the relative Picard functor
19	\(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) = \Pic(C \times_k T) / \pi_T^* \Pic(T)\)
20	on the category of \(k\)-schemes. Representing \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) requires the line-bundle
21	pullback construction on a relative curve \(C \times_k T \to T\), which in turn rests on a
22	\emph{relative scheme} construction on a quasi-coherent sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras
23	\(\mathcal{A}\), the \emph{relative spectrum}
24	\(\pi : \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}) \to X\). This chapter packages the construction
25	of \(\underline{\Spec}_X(-)\) and its core properties --- universal property and affine
26	base --- as the foundational A.1.a building block. The relative Picard
27	functor itself (A.1.c), the line-bundle pullback functor on a product (A.1.b), and the
28	identity-component / dimension theory of \(J\) (A.3) live in separate sibling chapters
29	(\S~``Out of scope'' below).
30	
31	% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Section ``Relative spectrum via glueing'' and ``Relative
32	% spectrum as a functor'' (tags 01LL, 01LO, 01LQ, 01LR, 01LS, available locally at
33	% references/stacks-constructions.tex L309-L720). The Hartshorne reference is
34	% [Hartshorne], II Exercise 5.17 (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf).

36	\section{Quasi-coherent sheaves of algebras}
37	\label{sec:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}
38	
39	\begin{definition}\leanok
40	[Quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebra]
41	  \label{def:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}
42	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.QcohAlgebra}
43	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Section 01LL ``Relative spectrum via glueing'',
44	  % Situation ``situation-relative-spec''
45	  % (read from references/stacks-constructions.tex, L312-L318).
46	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
47	  % "Here \(S\) is a scheme, and \(\mathcal{A}\) is a quasi-coherent
48	  %  \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-algebra. This means that \(\mathcal{A}\) is a
49	  %  sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-algebras which is quasi-coherent as an
50	  %  \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-module."
51	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 01LL (situation-relative-spec).}
52	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. A \emph{quasi-coherent sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras} is a
53	  sheaf \(\mathcal{A}\) of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras (i.e. an \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module
54	  equipped with \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-linear multiplication and unit morphisms satisfying
55	  associativity, commutativity, and the unit law as sheaf morphisms) whose underlying
56	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module is quasi-coherent. We write \(\mathrm{QcohAlg}(X)\) for the
57	  category of quasi-coherent sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras and
58	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebra morphisms.
59	\end{definition}

79	\section{The relative-spectrum scheme}
80	\label{sec:relspec_scheme}
81	
82	The construction is given affine-locally: on every affine open \(U = \Spec R \subseteq X\) one
83	sets \(\pi^{-1}(U) := \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U))\) regarded as an \(R\)-algebra, and the local pieces
84	glue compatibly because \(\mathcal{A}\) is quasi-coherent (the transition isomorphism
85	\(\mathcal{A}(U) \otimes_R S \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{A}(V)\) for an inclusion
86	\(V = \Spec S \subseteq U\) gives an open immersion of the corresponding Specs).
87	
88	\begin{theorem}\leanok
89	[Relative spectrum exists]
90	  \label{thm:relative_spec_exists}
91	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RelativeSpec}
92	  \uses{def:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}
93	
94	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 01LQ (lemma-glue-relative-spec)
95	  % (read from references/stacks-constructions.tex, L381-L405).
96	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
97	  % "In Situation REF.
98	  %  There exists a morphism of schemes
99	  %  \(\)
100	  %  \pi : \underline{\Spec}_S(\mathcal{A}) \longrightarrow S
101	  %  \(\)
102	  %  with the following properties:
103	  %  \begin{enumerate}
104	  %  \item for every affine open \(U \subset S\) there exists an isomorphism
105	  %  \(i_U : \pi^{-1}(U) \to \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U))\) over \(U\), and
106	  %  \item for \(U \subset U' \subset S\) affine open the composition
107	  %  \(\)
108	  %  \xymatrix{
109	  %  \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U)) \ar[r]^{i_U^{-1}} &
110	  %  \pi^{-1}(U) \ar[rr]^{inclusion} & &
111	  %  \pi^{-1}(U') \ar[r]^{i_{U'}} &
112	  %  \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U'))
113	  %  }
114	  %  \(\)
115	  %  is the open immersion of Lemma REF above.
116	  %  \end{enumerate}
117	  %  Moreover, \(\underline{\Spec}_S(\mathcal{A})\)
118	  %  is unique up to unique isomorphism over \(S\)."
119	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 01LQ (lemma-glue-relative-spec);
120	  cf.\ [Hartshorne], II~Ex.~5.17(a).}
121	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\mathcal{A}\) a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebra
122	  (\cref{def:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}). There exists a scheme
123	  \(\underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})\) together with an affine morphism
124	  \(\pi : \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}) \to X\) such that
125	  \begin{enumerate}
126	    \item for every affine open \(U \subseteq X\), there is an isomorphism
127	      \(i_U : \pi^{-1}(U) \xrightarrow{\sim} \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U))\) over \(U\);
128	    \item for \(U \subseteq U' \subseteq X\) both affine open, the composite
129	      $\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U)) \xrightarrow{i_U^{-1}} \pi^{-1}(U) \hookrightarrow
130	      \pi^{-1}(U') \xrightarrow{i_{U'}} \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U'))$
131	      coincides with the open immersion induced by the restriction
132	      \(\mathcal{A}(U') \to \mathcal{A}(U)\).
133	  \end{enumerate}
134	  The pair \((\underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}), \pi)\) is unique up to unique isomorphism
135	  over \(X\).
136	\end{theorem}
137	
138	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Follows immediately from
139	% Lemmas REF,
140	% REF, and
141	% REF.
142	% Uniqueness is stated in the last sentence of
143	% Lemma REF."
144	\begin{proof}
145	  
146	  
147	\leanok
148	  By the open-cover gluing principle for schemes (Mathlib
149	  \texttt{Scheme.GlueData} / \texttt{AffineScheme.glueOpens}) it suffices to specify
150	  affine local pieces \(\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U))\) for each affine open \(U \subseteq X\) and
151	  open-immersion transition data
152	  \(\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U)) \hookrightarrow \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U'))\) for \(U \subseteq U'\)
153	  satisfying the cocycle condition. The transition morphisms come from the ring maps
154	  \(\mathcal{A}(U') \to \mathcal{A}(U)\); quasi-coherence yields the cartesian square
155	  \(\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U)) = U \times_{U'} \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U'))\), so each transition is an
156	  open immersion. Transitivity (Stacks lemma-transitive-spec) gives the cocycle condition.
157	  Uniqueness of the glued scheme is the standard property of the gluing functor.
158	\end{proof}
159	
160	\begin{definition}
161	
162	\leanok
163	[Structure morphism of the relative spectrum]
164	  \label{def:relspec_structure_morphism}
165	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RelativeSpec.structureMorphism}
166	  \uses{thm:relative_spec_exists, def:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}
167	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\mathcal{A}\) a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebra.
168	  The \emph{structure morphism}
169	  \(\pi : \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}) \to X\) is the canonical projection of the
170	  relative spectrum to its base, obtained as the colimit descent of the affine-local
171	  structure morphisms \(\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U)) \to U\) over the directed affine open
172	  cover of \(X\). It is the morphism whose affineness and base-change behaviour are the
173	  subject of the universal-property and base-change results below.
174	\end{definition}
175	
176	\begin{proof}
177	
178	\leanok
179	  Constructed directly in Lean as the base morphism of the relative gluing data of
180	  \(\mathcal{A}\); this is the projection underlying \cref{thm:relative_spec_exists}.
181	\end{proof}

184	[Universal property of the relative spectrum]
185	  \label{thm:relative_spec_univ}
186	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RelativeSpec.UniversalProperty}
187	  % NOTE iter-283: removed the spurious `thm:relative_spec_base_change` from this
188	  % \uses. The universal property is the PRIMITIVE statement, proved by reducing
189	  % to the affine-base case and gluing (Lean body of `UniversalProperty` uses

195	  \uses{thm:relative_spec_exists, thm:relative_spec_affine_base,

197

257	  % The iter-179 Block A refactor replaces the iter-176 placeholder bodies with
258	  % the Mathlib values ``(AffineZariskiSite.relativeGluingData _).glued`` /
259	  % ``.toBase``; iter-179 Lane B closes the Lean ``UniversalProperty`` proof

266	  % iter-180+). Consumers may now rely on the affineness conclusion, but the
267	  % Yoneda-bijection witness is still deferred.
268	  Following Stacks tag 01LQ (proof of lemma-spec): the functor \(F\) is a Zariski sheaf
269	  because morphisms of schemes and \(\mathcal{O}\)-algebra maps both glue under open covers.
270	  Choose an affine open cover \(X = \bigcup U_i\). The subfunctor
271	  \(F_i \subseteq F\) of pairs \((f, \varphi)\) with \(f(T) \subseteq U_i\) is representable by
272	  \(\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U_i))\) via the affine-base case
273	  (\cref{thm:relative_spec_affine_base}) together with the
274	  restriction identification \(F_i \cong F_{U_i,\,\mathcal{A}|_{U_i}}\).
275	  Each \(F_i \hookrightarrow F\) is an open
276	  subfunctor (pull back \(U_i\) along \(f\)). The \(F_i\) jointly cover \(F\) since the \(U_i\)
277	  cover \(X\). The gluing of representable open subfunctors produces a representing scheme,
278	  which is canonically isomorphic to the glued scheme \(\underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})\)
279	  of \cref{thm:relative_spec_exists}.

282	\section{Affine base}
283	\label{sec:relspec_affine}
284	
285	\begin{theorem}\leanok
286	[Relative spectrum over an affine base]
287	  \label{thm:relative_spec_affine_base}
288	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RelativeSpec.affine_base_iff}
289	  \uses{def:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}
290	  % NOTE (iter-173 review): the iter-173 file-skeleton encodes this as the
291	  % weaker ``IsAffine ((Spec R).RelativeSpec 𝒜)'' rather than the canonical
292	  % iso ``Spec_X(𝒜) ≅ Spec(Γ(X, 𝒜))'' pinned by this prose. The Lean name
293	  % ``affine_base_iff'' is also misleading (the encoded type is not an iff).
294	  % Iter-174+ refines to the iff-with-Γ statement once Γ : QcohAlgebra → CommRingCat
295	  % is available.
296	  
297	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 01LO (lemma-spec-affine)
298	  % (read from references/stacks-constructions.tex, L491-L545).
299	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
300	  % "In Situation REF.
301	  %  Let \(F\) be the functor associated to \((S, \mathcal{A})\) above.
302	  %  If \(S\) is affine, then \(F\) is representable by the
303	  %  affine scheme \(\Spec(\Gamma(S, \mathcal{A}))\)."
304	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 01LO (lemma-spec-affine).}
305	  Let \(X = \Spec R\) be an affine scheme and let \(\mathcal{A}\) be a quasi-coherent
306	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebra. Set \(A := \Gamma(X, \mathcal{A})\), regarded as an
307	  \(R\)-algebra. Then there is a canonical isomorphism of \(X\)-schemes
308	  \[
309	    \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}) \;\;\cong\;\; \Spec(A).
310	  \]
311	  In particular, on an affine base the relative spectrum reduces to the absolute
312	  spectrum of the global sections.
313	\end{theorem}

321	% S = \Spec(R).
322	% \(\)
323	% We have
324	% \(f_{univ}^*\mathcal{A} =  \widetilde{A \otimes_R A}\)
325	% by Schemes, Lemma REF.
326	% Hence there is a canonical map
327	% \(\)
328	% \varphi_{univ} :
329	% f_{univ}^*\mathcal{A} = \widetilde{A \otimes_R A}
330	% \longrightarrow
331	% \widetilde A = \mathcal{O}_{\Spec(A)}
332	% \(\)
333	% coming from the \(A\)-module map \(A \otimes_R A \to A\),
334	% \(a \otimes a' \mapsto aa'\). We claim that the pair
335	% \((f_{univ}, \varphi_{univ})\) represents \(F\) in this case."

337	  
338	  \leanok
339	  % NOTE (iter-179 review): supersedes the iter-176 placeholder-discharge note.
340	  % The iter-176 ``change IsAffine (Spec R); inferInstance`` form is now stale
341	  % — iter-179 Lane B closes via ``UniversalProperty 𝒜`` + ``isAffine_of_isAffineHom``
342	  % against the iter-179 Mathlib-backed ``RelativeSpec``/``structureMorphism``
343	  % bodies. The Lean type remains the strictly weaker ``IsAffine`` rather than
344	  % the canonical iso ``Spec_X(𝒜) ≅ Spec(Γ(X,𝒜))'' named below (iter-174+
345	  % commitment; still pending iter-180+).
346	  Write \(X = \Spec R\) and \(A = \Gamma(X, \mathcal{A})\). Quasi-coherence of
347	  \(\mathcal{A}\) gives \(\mathcal{A} = \widetilde{A}\) as \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
348	  (with the \(R\)-algebra structure on \(A\) inducing the algebra structure on
349	  \(\widetilde A\)). The ring map \(R \to A\) gives a morphism
350	  \(f_{\mathrm{univ}} : \Spec A \to \Spec R = X\) and a canonical
351	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\Spec A}\)-algebra map
352	  $\varphi_{\mathrm{univ}} : f_{\mathrm{univ}}^* \mathcal{A} = \widetilde{A \otimes_R A}
353	  \to \mathcal{O}_{\Spec A} = \widetilde A$ given by multiplication
354	  \(a \otimes a' \mapsto aa'\). We verify \((\Spec A, f_{\mathrm{univ}}, \varphi_{\mathrm{univ}})\)
355	  represents \(F\) on the affine base: given a test scheme \(T\) and a pair
356	  \((f, \varphi)\), the composite
357	  $A = \Gamma(X, \mathcal{A}) \xrightarrow{f^\sharp}
358	  \Gamma(T, f^*\mathcal{A}) \xrightarrow{\varphi} \Gamma(T, \mathcal{O}_T)$

362	  \texttt{Scheme.Hom.toSpec}). One checks this assignment inverts the universal map
363	  \((f, \varphi) \mapsto a\) defined by \(f = f_{\mathrm{univ}} \circ a\),
364	  \(\varphi = a^* \varphi_{\mathrm{univ}}\).
